words.definition: Belief in the existence of a god or gods.
words.example: Theism takes many culturally distinct forms.

words.definition: Disbelief in the existence of God.
words.example: Atheism is not the same as anti-theism.

words.definition: The view that the existence of God is unknown or unknowable.
words.example: Agnosticism is a position on knowledge, not belief.

words.definition: Belief in one god.
words.example: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam are monotheisms.

words.definition: Belief in many gods.
The doctrine that God is identical with the universe.
words.example: Spinoza is often called a pantheist.

words.definition: The belief that God interpenetrates the universe but also transcends it.
words.example: Panentheism distinguishes itself from pantheism by transcendence.

words.definition: The belief in a creator who does not intervene in the universe.
words.example: Deism was influential among Enlightenment thinkers.

words.definition: A rationalist outlook attaching prime importance to human matters.
The view that reason is the chief source of knowledge.
The view that knowledge comes primarily from sensory experience.
The view that reality is fundamentally mental or spiritual.
The view that nothing exists except matter and its interactions.
The view that mind and matter are fundamentally distinct.
words.example: Cartesian dualism separates the mental from the physical.

words.definition: The view that reality is a single substance.
The view that reality consists of many kinds of substances.
words.example: William James defended a philosophical pluralism.

words.definition: A philosophy emphasising individual existence, freedom, and choice.
words.example: Existentialism holds that we create meaning through action.

words.definition: The rejection of all religious and moral principles.
words.example: Nihilism denies any inherent meaning to life.

words.definition: The belief that the search for meaning is futile in a meaningless universe.
words.example: Camus's absurdism confronts life without appeal to hope.

words.definition: An ancient school teaching endurance and virtue as the highest good.
words.example: Stoicism has enjoyed a modern revival.

words.definition: The philosophy that pleasure, especially mental tranquillity, is the highest good.
words.example: Epicureanism is often misread as mere hedonism.

words.definition: An ancient school valuing virtue and self-sufficiency over convention.
The philosophical position that certain knowledge is impossible.
words.example: Ancient skepticism suspended judgement on all matters.

words.definition: The doctrine that actions are right in proportion to their promotion of happiness.
words.example: Utilitarianism weighs consequences for the greatest number.

words.definition: Ethics based on adherence to rules or duties.
words.example: Kantian deontology grounds morality in universal duties.

words.definition: The view that the morality of an action depends on its outcomes.
words.example: Consequentialism includes utilitarianism as its main branch.

words.definition: An approach emphasising character and virtue over rules or consequences.
words.example: Aristotelian virtue ethics has been widely revived.

words.definition: The view that truth or morality is relative to individuals or cultures.
The view that certain truths or values are universal and unchanging.
The doctrine that all events are determined by prior causes.
The doctrine that human agents can choose independently of prior causes.
words.example: Debates over free will span philosophy and neuroscience.

words.definition: The view that free will and determinism are compatible.
words.example: Compatibilism reconciles moral responsibility with causal determinism.

words.definition: The view that only one's own mind is certain to exist.
words.example: Solipsism is difficult to argue against on its own terms.

words.definition: The philosophical study of the structures of experience.
words.example: Husserlian phenomenology brackets the natural attitude.

words.definition: The view that only scientific knowledge is authentic.
words.example: Logical positivism dominated early twentieth-century philosophy.

words.definition: The philosophy that judges truth by practical consequences.
words.example: American pragmatism emphasises what beliefs do for us.

words.definition: A method of analysing culture by relations within a system.
words.example: Structuralism shaped twentieth-century linguistics and anthropology.

words.definition: A movement challenging the assumptions of structuralism.
A late-twentieth-century movement skeptical of grand narratives.
words.example: Postmodernism blurs high and low culture.

words.definition: A social and economic philosophy based on the writings of Karl Marx.
words.example: Marxism analyses history through class struggle.

words.definition: An economic system based on private ownership of production.
words.example: Capitalism drives innovation and inequality alike.

words.definition: A political and economic theory of collective ownership.
words.example: Democratic socialism defends welfare within a market.

words.definition: A political philosophy opposing all coerced authority.
A political philosophy prioritising individual liberty.
A political philosophy valuing tradition and gradual change.
A political philosophy valuing liberty and equality.
A movement advocating women's rights and gender equality.
words.example: Feminism has produced several distinct waves.

words.definition: The view that all humans belong to a single moral community.
The view that only natural laws operate in the world.
The belief that all events are predetermined and inevitable.
words.example: Fatalism can conflict with even a soft determinism.

words.definition: Life has no inherent meaning; humans seek it anyway.
Priority of beauty over other values.
Believes in a deity while unsure it can be known.
words.example: Agnostic theism blends faith and doubt.

words.definition: Regard for the well-being of others.
Stateless society with market anarchy.
Workers' unions replace state.
Denies mind-independent reality.
Aristotle-based philosophy.
Focus on observable behaviour.
Teachings on suffering and non-self.
Descartes' mind-body dualism.
Ethical system of Confucius.
Knowledge is socially constructed.
Morality from hypothetical contract.
Rejection of conventional desires.
Way of Dao; harmony with nature.
Unravelling of textual meaning.
Compatibilist free will.
words.example: Soft determinism seeks a middle path.

words.definition: Morality from divine command.
words.example: Divine command theory grounds many religions.

words.definition: All persons deserve equal rights.
People always act from self-interest.
Self-interest is morally right.
New properties arise from complexity.
External world is knowable through senses.
Ethical concern for nature.
Mind is a by-product of brain.
Truth varies by framework.
Things have essential natures.
Meaning depends on external world.
Knowledge rests on basic beliefs.
Pleasure is the highest good.
Hegel's dialectical idealism.
History determines meaning.
Wholes exceed sum of parts.
Ethics without deity.
Divinity is within the world.
Theories are tools, not truths.
Meaning is in the mind.
Moral truths grasped by intuition.
Kant's duty-based ethics.
Law separated from morality.
Only verifiable claims meaningful.
Ruthless political realism.
words.example: Machiavellianism advises the ends over the means.

words.definition: Objective moral facts exist.
Revival of Plato's ideas.
Rand's rational self-interest ethics.
All religions share timeless truths.
Multiple valid moral values.
Complex things reduce to simple parts.
Judge rules by overall utility.
